```{r}
  rmodel_moderation_game <- rlmer(
    spane_game_balance ~ player_type * hours + (1|country),
    data = pvz_df_clustered
  )

  rmodel_moderation_enjoyment <- rlmer(
    enjoyment ~ player_type * hours + (1|country),
    data = pvz_df_clustered
  )
  
  # Save models to cache
  save_model(rmodel_moderation_game, "rmodel_moderation_game")
  save_model(rmodel_moderation_enjoyment, "rmodel_moderation_enjoyment")
} else {
  # Load cached models
  rmodel_moderation_game <- load_model("rmodel_moderation_game")
  rmodel_moderation_enjoyment <- load_model("rmodel_moderation_enjoyment")
}

# Display moderation models side-by-side using modelsummary
modelsummary(
  list("Well-being (SPANE)" = rmodel_moderation_game, "Enjoyment" = rmodel_moderation_enjoyment),
  estimate = "{estimate} ({std.error})",
  statistic = "t = {statistic}",
  gof_omit = "AIC|BIC|RMSE|Log",
  coef_rename = c(
    "(Intercept)" = "Intercept",
    "player_typeElite Allround Players" = "Elite Allround Players",
    "player_typeCasual PVP Players" = "Casual PVP Players",
    "hours" = "Hours Played",
    "player_typeElite Allround Players:hours" = "Elite Allround × Hours",
    "player_typeCasual PVP Players:hours" = "Casual PVP × Hours"
  ),
  title = "Moderation Models: Play Style × Hours Interaction",
  notes = "Reference category for play style: Casual PVE Players. Country included as random effect."
)

# Extract coefficients for inline reporting
coefs_mod_game <- coef(summary(rmodel_moderation_game))
coefs_mod_enjoyment <- coef(summary(rmodel_moderation_enjoyment))
```

To test whether the playtime–outcome association differed across playstyles, we added player type × hours interaction terms to the models (Appendix 4). Each interaction coefficient represents the difference in the playtime slope for a given playstyle relative to Casual PVE Players. None of the interaction terms reached significance: for well-being, neither Casual PVP Players (Δb = `r round(coefs_mod_game["player_typeCasual PVP Players:hours", "Estimate"], 2)`, SE = `r round(coefs_mod_game["player_typeCasual PVP Players:hours", "Std. Error"], 2)`, t = `r round(coefs_mod_game["player_typeCasual PVP Players:hours", "t value"], 2)`) nor Elite Allround Players (Δb = `r round(coefs_mod_game["player_typeElite Allround Players:hours", "Estimate"], 2)`, SE = `r round(coefs_mod_game["player_typeElite Allround Players:hours", "Std. Error"], 2)`, t = `r round(coefs_mod_game["player_typeElite Allround Players:hours", "t value"], 2)`) had a playtime slope that differed from the reference group. The same held for enjoyment (Casual PVP: Δb = `r round(coefs_mod_enjoyment["player_typeCasual PVP Players:hours", "Estimate"], 2)`, SE = `r round(coefs_mod_enjoyment["player_typeCasual PVP Players:hours", "Std. Error"], 2)`, t = `r round(coefs_mod_enjoyment["player_typeCasual PVP Players:hours", "t value"], 2)`; Elite Allround: Δb = `r round(coefs_mod_enjoyment["player_typeElite Allround Players:hours", "Estimate"], 2)`, SE = `r round(coefs_mod_enjoyment["player_typeElite Allround Players:hours", "Std. Error"], 2)`, t = `r round(coefs_mod_enjoyment["player_typeElite Allround Players:hours", "t value"], 2)`). Baseline playstyle differences in intercepts remained in the interaction models, particularly for Casual PVP Players (well-being: b = `r round(coefs_mod_game["player_typeCasual PVP Players", "Estimate"], 2)`, t = `r round(coefs_mod_game["player_typeCasual PVP Players", "t value"], 2)`; enjoyment: b = `r round(coefs_mod_enjoyment["player_typeCasual PVP Players", "Estimate"], 2)`, t = `r round(coefs_mod_enjoyment["player_typeCasual PVP Players", "t value"], 2)`).

```{r}
#| label: wellbeing-anova
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| include: false

# Run ANOVAs to test for significant differences between player types
wellbeing_vars <- c("spane_game_balance", "enjoyment")

# Store ANOVA results and models
anova_results <- list()
anova_models <- list()

for (var in wellbeing_vars) {
  formula <- as.formula(paste(var, "~ player_type"))
  anova_model <- aov(formula, data = pvz_df_clustered)
  anova_models[[var]] <- anova_model
  anova_results[[var]] <- broom::tidy(anova_model)
  anova_results[[var]]$variable <- var
  
  # Add effect size
  anova_results[[var]]$eta_squared <- effectsize::eta_squared(anova_model)[["Eta2"]]
  
  # Add residual df
  anova_results[[var]]$df_residual <- summary(anova_model)[[1]]["Residuals", "Df"]
}

# Combine all results
all_anova_results <- bind_rows(anova_results)

# Display results in a nice table
all_anova_results |> 
  filter(term == "player_type") |>
  select(Variable = variable, df, SS = sumsq, F = statistic, p = p.value, `η²` = eta_squared) |>
  mutate(
    p = case_when(
      p < 0.001 ~ "< .001^†^",
      p < 0.05 ~ paste0(sprintf("%.3f", round(p, 3)), "^†^"),
      TRUE ~ sprintf("%.3f", round(p, 3))
    ),
    `η²` = round(`η²`, 3)
  )

# Post-hoc tests for variables with significant differences
posthoc_results <- list()

for (var in wellbeing_vars) {
  if (summary(anova_models[[var]])[[1]]["player_type", "Pr(>F)"] < 0.05) {
    posthoc <- TukeyHSD(anova_models[[var]])
    posthoc_results[[var]] <- broom::tidy(posthoc)
  }
}

# Extract key statistics for inline reporting
wellbeing_anova <- all_anova_results |> 
  filter(term == "player_type" & variable == "spane_game_balance")
enjoyment_anova <- all_anova_results |> 
  filter(term == "player_type" & variable == "enjoyment")

# Extract post-hoc p-values
wellbeing_posthoc <- posthoc_results[["spane_game_balance"]]
enjoyment_posthoc <- posthoc_results[["enjoyment"]]

# Get specific comparisons (TukeyHSD tidy output uses 'contrast' column)
wb_pvp_pve <- wellbeing_posthoc |> 
  filter(grepl("Casual PVP.*Casual PVE|Casual PVE.*Casual PVP", contrast))
enj_pvp_pve <- enjoyment_posthoc |> 
  filter(grepl("Casual PVP.*Casual PVE|Casual PVE.*Casual PVP", contrast))
enj_pvp_elite <- enjoyment_posthoc |> 
  filter(grepl("Casual PVP.*Elite|Elite.*Casual PVP", contrast))
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-anova-boxplots
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of well-being outcomes by play style. Panel A shows game-specific well-being (SPANE balance score); Panel B shows enjoyment. Boxes represent interquartile ranges with median lines; whiskers extend to 1.5 × IQR. Individual points show outliers."
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 5
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Create boxplot for game-specific well-being
p_wellbeing <- ggplot(pvz_df_clustered, aes(x = player_type, y = spane_game_balance, fill = player_type)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, outlier.shape = 21) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.2, alpha = 0.2, size = 1) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(
    title = "A. Game-Specific Well-being",
    x = NULL,
    y = "SPANE Balance Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 15, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
  )

# Create boxplot for enjoyment
p_enjoyment <- ggplot(pvz_df_clustered, aes(x = player_type, y = enjoyment, fill = player_type)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, outlier.shape = 21) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.2, alpha = 0.2, size = 1) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(
    title = "B. Enjoyment",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Enjoyment Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 15, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
  )

# Combine plots
p_wellbeing + p_enjoyment
```

```{r}
#| label: dynamic-results-text
#| echo: false

# Function to format p-value for inline text
format_p_inline <- function(p) {
  if (length(p) == 0 || is.na(p[1])) return("N/A")
  p <- p[1]  # Take first value if vector
  if (p < .001) return("< .001")
  sprintf("= %.3f", p)
}
```

These playstyle differences were corroborated by one-way ANOVAs (well-being: *F*(`r wellbeing_anova$df`, `r wellbeing_anova$df_residual`) = `r sprintf("%.2f", wellbeing_anova$statistic)`, *p* `r format_p_inline(wellbeing_anova$p.value)`, η² = `r sprintf("%.3f", wellbeing_anova$eta_squared)`; enjoyment: *F*(`r enjoyment_anova$df`, `r enjoyment_anova$df_residual`) = `r sprintf("%.2f", enjoyment_anova$statistic)`, *p* `r format_p_inline(enjoyment_anova$p.value)`, η² = `r sprintf("%.3f", enjoyment_anova$eta_squared)`) and Tukey HSD post-hoc tests, which confirmed that Casual PVP Players scored lower than Casual PVE Players on both well-being (*p* `r format_p_inline(wb_pvp_pve$adj.p.value)`) and enjoyment (*p* `r format_p_inline(enj_pvp_pve$adj.p.value)`), and lower than Elite Allround Players on enjoyment (*p* `r format_p_inline(enj_pvp_elite$adj.p.value)`) (@fig-anova-boxplots).


```{r}
#| label: simple-slopes-functions
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| include: false

# Function to calculate simple slopes for all player types
calculate_all_simple_slopes <- function(model, outcome_name) {
  # For robust mixed models, extract coefficients manually
  coefs <- fixef(model)
  vcov_matrix <- vcov(model)
  
  # Get player types
  player_types <- levels(pvz_df_clustered$player_type)
  
  # Base effect of hours (for reference level)
  hours_effect <- coefs["hours"]
  hours_se <- sqrt(vcov_matrix["hours", "hours"])
  
  # Create results dataframe
  results <- data.frame(
    player_type = player_types[1],  # reference level
    estimate = hours_effect,
    std.error = hours_se,
    statistic = hours_effect / hours_se,
    p.value = 2 * pt(abs(hours_effect / hours_se), 
                    df = nrow(pvz_df_clustered) - length(coefs), 
                    lower.tail = FALSE),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
  
  # Calculate for other player types
  for (i in 2:length(player_types)) {
    pt <- player_types[i]
    interaction_term <- paste0("player_type", pt, ":hours")
    
    if (interaction_term %in% names(coefs)) {
      # Calculate slope
      slope <- hours_effect + coefs[interaction_term]
      
      # Calculate SE using delta method (simplified)
      var_hours <- vcov_matrix["hours", "hours"]
      var_inter <- vcov_matrix[interaction_term, interaction_term]
      cov_hours_inter <- vcov_matrix["hours", interaction_term]
      slope_se <- sqrt(var_hours + var_inter + 2*cov_hours_inter)
      
      # t-statistic and p-value
      t_stat <- slope / slope_se
      p_val <- 2 * pt(abs(t_stat), df = nrow(pvz_df_clustered) - length(coefs), lower.tail = FALSE)
      
      # Add to results
      results <- rbind(results, data.frame(
        player_type = pt,
        estimate = slope,
        std.error = slope_se,
        statistic = t_stat,
        p.value = p_val,
        stringsAsFactors = FALSE
      ))
    }
  }
  
  # Add confidence intervals and formatting
  results <- results %>%
    mutate(
      outcome = outcome_name,
      conf.low = estimate - 1.96 * std.error,
      conf.high = estimate + 1.96 * std.error,
      significant = p.value < 0.05,
      sig = case_when(
        p.value < 0.001 ~ "^†^",
        p.value < 0.01 ~ "^†^",
        p.value < 0.05 ~ "^†^",
        TRUE ~ ""
      )
    )
  
  return(results)
}

# Function to create interaction plots for all player types
create_interaction_plots <- function(model, outcome_name) {
  # Extract fixed effects
  fixed_effects <- fixef(model)
  
  # Get player types
  player_types <- levels(pvz_df_clustered$player_type)
  
  # Create a grid of values for hours
  hours_range <- range(pvz_df_clustered$hours, na.rm = TRUE)
  hours_grid <- seq(from = hours_range[1], to = hours_range[2], length.out = 100)
  
  # Get the coefficient for hours (main effect)
  hours_coef <- fixed_effects["hours"]
  
  # Create a dataframe to store predictions
  predictions <- data.frame()
  
  # For each player type, calculate predicted values across hours range
  for (i in 1:length(player_types)) {
    pt <- player_types[i]
    
    # Calculate intercept
    if (i == 1) {
      # Reference level
      intercept <- fixed_effects["(Intercept)"]
      slope <- hours_coef
    } else {
      # Other levels
      intercept <- fixed_effects["(Intercept)"] + fixed_effects[paste0("player_type", pt)]
      interaction_term <- paste0("player_type", pt, ":hours")
      slope <- hours_coef + fixed_effects[interaction_term]
    }
    
    # Calculate predictions
    pred <- intercept + slope * hours_grid
    
    # Add to predictions dataframe
    predictions <- rbind(predictions, data.frame(
      player_type = pt,
      hours = hours_grid,
      predicted = pred
    ))
  }
  
  # Create the plot
  p <- ggplot(predictions, aes(x = hours, y = predicted, color = player_type)) +
    geom_line(size = 1.2) +
    geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = predicted - 1.96 * 0.5, ymax = predicted + 1.96 * 0.5, fill = player_type), alpha = 0.2) +
    labs(
      title = paste("Interaction Plot for", outcome_name),
      x = "Hours Played",
      y = outcome_name,
      color = "Player Type",
      fill = "Player Type"
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
      legend.position = "bottom",
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
    ) +
    scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
    scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2")
  
  return(p)
}

# Function to create Johnson-Neyman plot
create_jn_plot <- function(model, moderator = "player_type", predictor = "hours", outcome_name) {
  # For rlmerMod models, we'll use a custom approach
  # This function creates a simplified version of Johnson-Neyman plot
  
  # Extract fixed effects
  fixed_effects <- fixef(model)
  vcov_matrix <- vcov(model)
  
  # Get player types
  player_types <- levels(pvz_df_clustered$player_type)
  
  # Create a dataframe to store slopes and confidence intervals
  jn_data <- data.frame(
    player_type = player_types,
    slope = numeric(length(player_types)),
    se = numeric(length(player_types)),
    lower_ci = numeric(length(player_types)),
    upper_ci = numeric(length(player_types)),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
  
  # Get the coefficient for hours (main effect)
  hours_coef <- fixed_effects["hours"]
  hours_se <- sqrt(vcov_matrix["hours", "hours"])
  
  # For each player type, calculate slope and CI
  for (i in 1:length(player_types)) {
    pt <- player_types[i]
    
    if (i == 1) {
      # Reference level
      jn_data$slope[i] <- hours_coef
      jn_data$se[i] <- hours_se
    } else {
      # Other levels
      interaction_term <- paste0("player_type", pt, ":hours")
      slope <- hours_coef + fixed_effects[interaction_term]
      
      # Calculate SE using delta method (simplified)
      var_hours <- vcov_matrix["hours", "hours"]
      var_inter <- vcov_matrix[interaction_term, interaction_term]
      cov_hours_inter <- vcov_matrix["hours", interaction_term]
      slope_se <- sqrt(var_hours + var_inter + 2*cov_hours_inter)
      
      jn_data$slope[i] <- slope
      jn_data$se[i] <- slope_se
    }
    
    # Calculate 95% confidence intervals
    jn_data$lower_ci[i] <- jn_data$slope[i] - 1.96 * jn_data$se[i]
    jn_data$upper_ci[i] <- jn_data$slope[i] + 1.96 * jn_data$se[i]
  }
  
  # Determine significance
  jn_data$significant <- (jn_data$lower_ci > 0) | (jn_data$upper_ci < 0)
  
  # Create the plot
  p <- ggplot(jn_data, aes(x = player_type, y = slope, color = significant)) +
    geom_point(size = 3) +
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lower_ci, ymax = upper_ci), width = 0.2) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
    labs(
      title = paste("Conditional Effect of", predictor, "on", outcome_name),
      subtitle = "by Player Type with 95% Confidence Intervals",
      x = "Player Type",
      y = paste("Effect of", predictor),
      color = "Significant"
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
      legend.position = "bottom",
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
    ) +
    scale_color_manual(values = c("gray50", "blue"))
  
  return(p)
}
```

```{r}
#| label: wellbeing-simple-slopes
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| include: false

# Calculate simple slopes for game-specific well-being model
wellbeing_slopes <- calculate_all_simple_slopes(rmodel_moderation_game, "Game-Specific Well-being")

# Create a formatted table for game-specific well-being simple slopes
wellbeing_slopes_table <- wellbeing_slopes %>%
  mutate(
    estimate = round(estimate, 3),
    std.error = round(std.error, 3),
    statistic = round(statistic, 3),
    p.value = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ paste0("< .001", sig),
      p.value < 0.05 ~ paste0(sprintf("%.3f", round(p.value, 3)), sig),
      TRUE ~ sprintf("%.3f", round(p.value, 3))
    ),
    ci = paste0("[", round(conf.low, 3), ", ", round(conf.high, 3), "]")
  ) %>%
  select(`Player Type` = player_type, b = estimate, SE = std.error, t = statistic, p = p.value, `95% CI` = ci)
```

```{r}
#| label: enjoyment-simple-slopes
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| include: false

# Calculate simple slopes for enjoyment model
enjoyment_slopes <- calculate_all_simple_slopes(rmodel_moderation_enjoyment, "Intrinsic Enjoyment")

# Create a formatted table for enjoyment simple slopes
enjoyment_slopes_table <- enjoyment_slopes %>%
  mutate(
    estimate = round(estimate, 3),
    std.error = round(std.error, 3),
    statistic = round(statistic, 3),
    p.value = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ paste0("< .001", sig),
      p.value < 0.05 ~ paste0(sprintf("%.3f", round(p.value, 3)), sig),
      TRUE ~ sprintf("%.3f", round(p.value, 3))
    ),
    ci = paste0("[", round(conf.low, 3), ", ", round(conf.high, 3), "]")
  ) %>%
  select(`Player Type` = player_type, b = estimate, SE = std.error, t = statistic, p = p.value, `95% CI` = ci)
```

```{r}
#| label: wellbeing-pairwise-comparisons
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| include: false

# Create pairwise comparison of slopes for game-specific well-being
wellbeing_pairs <- data.frame(player_type1 = character(),
                        player_type2 = character(),
                        difference = numeric(),
                        std.error = numeric(),
                        statistic = numeric(),
                        p.value = numeric(),
                        stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

player_types <- levels(pvz_df_clustered$player_type)
for (i in 1:(length(player_types)-1)) {
  for (j in (i+1):length(player_types)) {
    pt1 <- player_types[i]
    pt2 <- player_types[j]
    
    # Get slopes and standard errors
    slope1 <- wellbeing_slopes$estimate[wellbeing_slopes$player_type == pt1]
    slope2 <- wellbeing_slopes$estimate[wellbeing_slopes$player_type == pt2]
    se1 <- wellbeing_slopes$std.error[wellbeing_slopes$player_type == pt1]
    se2 <- wellbeing_slopes$std.error[wellbeing_slopes$player_type == pt2]
    
    # Calculate difference and standard error
    diff <- slope1 - slope2
    diff_se <- sqrt(se1^2 + se2^2)  # Simplified approach, assumes independence
    
    # Calculate test statistic and p-value
    t_stat <- diff / diff_se
    p_val <- 2 * pt(abs(t_stat), df = nrow(pvz_df_clustered) - 10, lower.tail = FALSE)  # Approximate df
    
    # Add to results
    wellbeing_pairs <- rbind(wellbeing_pairs, data.frame(
      player_type1 = pt1,
      player_type2 = pt2,
      difference = diff,
      std.error = diff_se,
      statistic = t_stat,
      p.value = p_val,
      stringsAsFactors = FALSE
    ))
  }
}

# Format and display pairwise comparison table for subjective well-being
wellbeing_pairs <- wellbeing_pairs %>%
  mutate(
    sig = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ "^†^",
      p.value < 0.01 ~ "^†^",
      p.value < 0.05 ~ "^†^", 
      TRUE ~ ""
    ),
    difference = round(difference, 3),
    std.error = round(std.error, 3),
    statistic = round(statistic, 3),
    p.value = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ paste0("< .001", sig),
      p.value < 0.05 ~ paste0(sprintf("%.3f", round(p.value, 3)), sig),
      TRUE ~ sprintf("%.3f", round(p.value, 3))
    )
  ) |>
  rename(`Type 1` = player_type1, `Type 2` = player_type2, Diff = difference, SE = std.error, t = statistic, p = p.value)
```

```{r}
#| label: enjoyment-pairwise-comparisons
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| include: false

# Create pairwise comparison of slopes for enjoyment model
enjoyment_pairs <- data.frame(player_type1 = character(),
                              player_type2 = character(),
                              difference = numeric(),
                              std.error = numeric(),
                              statistic = numeric(),
                              p.value = numeric(),
                              stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

for (i in 1:(length(player_types)-1)) {
  for (j in (i+1):length(player_types)) {
    pt1 <- player_types[i]
    pt2 <- player_types[j]
    
    # Get slopes and standard errors
    slope1 <- enjoyment_slopes$estimate[enjoyment_slopes$player_type == pt1]
    slope2 <- enjoyment_slopes$estimate[enjoyment_slopes$player_type == pt2]
    se1 <- enjoyment_slopes$std.error[enjoyment_slopes$player_type == pt1]
    se2 <- enjoyment_slopes$std.error[enjoyment_slopes$player_type == pt2]
    
    # Calculate difference and standard error
    diff <- slope1 - slope2
    diff_se <- sqrt(se1^2 + se2^2)  # Simplified approach, assumes independence
    
    # Calculate test statistic and p-value
    t_stat <- diff / diff_se
    p_val <- 2 * pt(abs(t_stat), df = nrow(pvz_df_clustered) - 10, lower.tail = FALSE)  # Approximate df
    
    # Add to results
    enjoyment_pairs <- rbind(enjoyment_pairs, data.frame(
      player_type1 = pt1,
      player_type2 = pt2,
      difference = diff,
      std.error = diff_se,
      statistic = t_stat,
      p.value = p_val,
      stringsAsFactors = FALSE
    ))
  }
}

# Format and display pairwise comparison table for enjoyment model
enjoyment_pairs <- enjoyment_pairs %>%
  mutate(
    sig = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ "^†††^",
      p.value < 0.01 ~ "^††^",
      p.value < 0.05 ~ "^†^", 
      TRUE ~ ""
    ),
    difference = round(difference, 3),
    std.error = round(std.error, 3),
    statistic = round(statistic, 3),
    p.value = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ paste0("< .001", sig),
      p.value < 0.05 ~ paste0(sprintf("%.3f", round(p.value, 3)), sig),
      TRUE ~ sprintf("%.3f", round(p.value, 3))
    )
  ) |>
  rename(`Type 1` = player_type1, `Type 2` = player_type2, Diff = difference, SE = std.error, t = statistic, p = p.value)
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-interaction-plots
#| fig-cap: "Interaction plots showing the relationship between hours played and psychological outcomes across play styles. Left panel shows game-specific well-being (SPANE balance score); right panel shows enjoyment. Lines represent linear regression slopes with 95% confidence intervals. Casual PVE Players and Elite Allround Players show positive associations between playtime and outcomes, while Casual PVP Players show no significant relationship."
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 6
#| code-summary: "Interaction plots for moderation analysis"
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Create scatter plots for total playtime vs. outcomes across player types with APA-style formatting
wellbeing_plot <- ggplot(pvz_df_clustered, aes(x = hours, y = spane_game_balance, color = player_type)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, alpha = 0.2, fullrange = TRUE) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(-6, 6), breaks = seq(-6, 6, 2)) +
  labs(
    x = "Total Hours Played",
    y = "SPANE Score",
    color = "Play Style"
  ) +
  # Apply APA-style formatting
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold", hjust = 0),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10, hjust = 0),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 11),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 10),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.border = element_rect(fill = NA, color = "black", size = 0.5),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "white")
  )

enjoyment_plot <- ggplot(pvz_df_clustered, aes(x = hours, y = enjoyment, color = player_type)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, alpha = 0.2, fullrange = TRUE) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(1, 7), breaks = seq(1, 7, 1)) +
  labs(
    x = "Total Hours Played",
    y = "Enjoyment Score",
    color = "Play Style"
  ) +
  # Apply APA-style formatting
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold", hjust = 0),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10, hjust = 0),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 11),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 10),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.border = element_rect(fill = NA, color = "black", size = 0.5),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "white")
  )

# Combine the plots with common legend at bottom
ggarrange(wellbeing_plot, enjoyment_plot, ncol = 2, common.legend = TRUE, legend = "bottom")
```

```{r}
#| label: simple-slopes-inline-values
#| echo: false

# Extract simple slopes values for inline reporting
# Wellbeing slopes
wb_pve <- wellbeing_slopes |> filter(player_type == "Casual PVE Players")
wb_pvp <- wellbeing_slopes |> filter(player_type == "Casual PVP Players")
wb_elite <- wellbeing_slopes |> filter(player_type == "Elite Allround Players")

# Enjoyment slopes  
enj_pve <- enjoyment_slopes |> filter(player_type == "Casual PVE Players")
enj_pvp <- enjoyment_slopes |> filter(player_type == "Casual PVP Players")
enj_elite <- enjoyment_slopes |> filter(player_type == "Elite Allround Players")

# Wellbeing pairwise comparisons (need numeric version before formatting)
wb_pairs_numeric <- data.frame(player_type1 = character(),
                               player_type2 = character(),
                               difference = numeric(),
                               std.error = numeric(),
                               statistic = numeric(),
                               p.value = numeric(),
                               stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

for (i in 1:(length(player_types)-1)) {
  for (j in (i+1):length(player_types)) {
    pt1 <- player_types[i]
    pt2 <- player_types[j]
    slope1 <- wellbeing_slopes$estimate[wellbeing_slopes$player_type == pt1]
    slope2 <- wellbeing_slopes$estimate[wellbeing_slopes$player_type == pt2]
    se1 <- wellbeing_slopes$std.error[wellbeing_slopes$player_type == pt1]
    se2 <- wellbeing_slopes$std.error[wellbeing_slopes$player_type == pt2]
    diff <- slope1 - slope2
    diff_se <- sqrt(se1^2 + se2^2)
    t_stat <- diff / diff_se
    p_val <- 2 * pt(abs(t_stat), df = nrow(pvz_df_clustered) - 6, lower.tail = FALSE)
    wb_pairs_numeric <- bind_rows(wb_pairs_numeric, data.frame(
      player_type1 = pt1, player_type2 = pt2,
      difference = diff, std.error = diff_se, statistic = t_stat, p.value = p_val
    ))
  }
}

# Enjoyment pairwise comparisons
enj_pairs_numeric <- data.frame(player_type1 = character(),
                                player_type2 = character(),
                                difference = numeric(),
                                std.error = numeric(),
                                statistic = numeric(),
                                p.value = numeric(),
                                stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

for (i in 1:(length(player_types)-1)) {
  for (j in (i+1):length(player_types)) {
    pt1 <- player_types[i]
    pt2 <- player_types[j]
    slope1 <- enjoyment_slopes$estimate[enjoyment_slopes$player_type == pt1]
    slope2 <- enjoyment_slopes$estimate[enjoyment_slopes$player_type == pt2]
    se1 <- enjoyment_slopes$std.error[enjoyment_slopes$player_type == pt1]
    se2 <- enjoyment_slopes$std.error[enjoyment_slopes$player_type == pt2]
    diff <- slope1 - slope2
    diff_se <- sqrt(se1^2 + se2^2)
    t_stat <- diff / diff_se
    p_val <- 2 * pt(abs(t_stat), df = nrow(pvz_df_clustered) - 6, lower.tail = FALSE)
    enj_pairs_numeric <- bind_rows(enj_pairs_numeric, data.frame(
      player_type1 = pt1, player_type2 = pt2,
      difference = diff, std.error = diff_se, statistic = t_stat, p.value = p_val
    ))
  }
}

# Helper function to format p-values for inline text
fmt_p <- function(p) {
  if (p < 0.001) return("< .001")
  sprintf("= %.3f", p)
}
```

To further probe the interaction, simple slopes analyses decomposed the playtime–outcome association within each playstyle (@fig-interaction-plots). Only Elite Allround Players showed a significant positive slope for both well-being (b = `r round(wb_elite$estimate, 3)`, *p* `r fmt_p(wb_elite$p.value)`) and enjoyment (b = `r round(enj_elite$estimate, 3)`, *p* `r fmt_p(enj_elite$p.value)`). Slopes for Casual PVE Players (well-being: b = `r round(wb_pve$estimate, 3)`, *p* `r fmt_p(wb_pve$p.value)`; enjoyment: b = `r round(enj_pve$estimate, 3)`, *p* `r fmt_p(enj_pve$p.value)`) and Casual PVP Players (well-being: b = `r round(wb_pvp$estimate, 3)`, *p* `r fmt_p(wb_pvp$p.value)`; enjoyment: b = `r round(enj_pvp$estimate, 3)`, *p* `r fmt_p(enj_pvp$p.value)`) were not significant. Critically, pairwise comparisons of slopes revealed no significant differences between any pair of playstyles (all *p*s > .05), confirming that the playtime–outcome gradient does not differ meaningfully across groups. Taken together, these results indicate that playstyle is associated with different baseline levels of well-being and enjoyment but does not moderate the relationship between playtime and either outcome.

# Discussion

This study addressed three questions: whether distinct playstyles can be identified from in-game behavioural telemetry within a single game, whether players with different playstyles report different levels of well-being and enjoyment, and whether the association between playtime and these outcomes differs across playstyles. The findings clearly support the first two propositions but not the third. Three distinct playstyles were recoverable from behavioural telemetry, and these playstyles were associated with significant differences in baseline well-being and enjoyment. However, we found no evidence that playstyle moderated the playtime–outcome relationship.

We operationalised playing styles through behavioural telemetry rather than self-report player type measures for several methodological reasons. First, telemetry captures actual behaviour rather than self-reported preferences, avoiding inaccuracies due to social desirability and recall biases [@zendleFourGrandChallenges2023]. Second, telemetry provides objective, granular data about gameplay patterns over time. Third, recent evidence suggests self-reported player types lack temporal stability [@santosConsistencyGamificationUser2023], raising questions about their validity as trait-like predictors. Behavioural playstyles derived from telemetry thus better capture consistent patterns of game engagement, offering more ecologically valid characterisations of player-game interactions than potentially unstable self-reported preferences measured at a single timepoint.

The three playstyles identified through PAM clustering (Casual PVE Players, Elite Allround Players, and Casual PVP Players) are conceptually consistent with player segments previously identified in other shooter titles. @drachenGunsSwordsData2012 identified player types in *Battlefield 2: Bad Company 2* that parallel our casual and elite profiles, whilst @melhartYourGameplaySays2019 found comparable segments in *Tom Clancy's The Division*, including PVE-focused and competitively skilled players. The recurrence of these patterns across different games suggests that such typologies may capture fundamental approaches to gameplay rather than game-specific idiosyncrasies. This finding directly supports the heterogeneous exposures framework [@ballouHowVideoGames2025]: even within a single title, players encounter qualitively different game-centred exposures depending on the modes they select and the mechanics they engage with.

However, it remains an open question whether playstyles themselves show temporal stability or represent dynamic adaptations to changing contexts. Unlike player types, which are theorised as relatively stable individual differences yet demonstrate considerable instability [@santosConsistencyGamificationUser2023], playstyles may be better conceptualised as characteristic behavioural patterns that emerge from the interaction between player, game, and context. This framing accommodates the possibility that players' behavioural patterns might shift with skill development, game progression, life circumstances, or wellbeing states, whilst still providing meaningful characterisations of gameplay within specific periods and contexts.

```{r}
#| label: discussion-statistics
#| echo: false
# Extract coefficients for inline reporting
coef_pvp_wellbeing <- get_model_coef(rmodel_game, "player_typeCasual PVP Players")
coef_pvp_enjoyment <- get_model_coef(rmodel_enjoyment, "player_typeCasual PVP Players")
coef_elite_wellbeing <- get_model_coef(rmodel_game, "player_typeElite Allround Players")
coef_elite_enjoyment <- get_model_coef(rmodel_enjoyment, "player_typeElite Allround Players")
coef_hours_game <- get_model_coef(rmodel_moderation_game, "hours")
coef_hours_enjoyment <- get_model_coef(rmodel_moderation_enjoyment, "hours")
```

Our robust mixed-effects models and complementary ANOVAs converged on a consistent pattern: Casual PVE Players reported higher game-specific well-being and enjoyment than Casual PVP Players, while Elite Allround Players fell between the two groups on enjoyment but were also lower than Casual PVE Players on well-being. These baseline differences indicate that different styles of engagement with the same game are associated with different psychological experiences, even after controlling for playtime, age, and gender.

The higher well-being among Casual PVE Players is noteworthy given that Elite Allround Players demonstrated substantially higher performance metrics across all modes. If competence alone drove well-being, the most skilled players should report the most positive experiences. Prior work does suggest that competence satisfaction positively predicts well-being in gaming contexts [@klimmt2009a; @przybylskiMotivationalModelVideo2010; @tamboriniMediaEnjoymentNeed2011; @ryanHappinessHumanPotentials2001]. However, our findings suggest that the *context* in which competence is exercised matters as well, consistent with the IMP's emphasis on both mechanics and context as jointly shaping player experience [@elsonMoreStoriesButtons2014]. Cooperative PVE environments may afford a subjective experience that is more exploratory than competitive PVP modes, potentially increasing perceived autonomy over how to engage with game content. This interpretation is speculative and requires direct measurement of need satisfaction across playstyles in future work, but it aligns with the broader pattern that *how* players engage with a game is more informative for average well-being than *how much* they play.

Contrary to our expectations, we found no evidence that playstyle moderates the association between playtime and well-being or enjoyment. Interaction terms were consistently non-significant, and pairwise comparisons of simple slopes confirmed that the playtime gradient did not differ across playstyles. Although Elite Allround Players showed the only significant positive simple slope, this did not translate into a significant difference from the slopes of other groups.

This null finding has important implications for the playtime debate. Recent large-scale digital trace studies have established that playtime alone is not meaningfully associated with well-being for most players [@ballouOpenPlayLongitudinal2025; @johannesVideoGamePlay2021; @vuorreTimeSpentPlaying2022]. One plausible explanation for these aggregate null findings is that playtime effects are heterogeneous—positive for some styles of play, negative for others, and averaging to near-zero overall. Our results do not support this explanation; the playtime–well-being slope was consistently small and did not vary by playstyle. The present data therefore cannot be used to argue that the playtime paradigm is inadequate because it overlooks differential playtime effects across player types.

Perhaps the more parsimonious interpretation is simply that playtime is not a meaningful determinant of well-being in the first place, not because the relationship is masked by heterogeneity, but because time spent playing is too blunt a measure to capture what matters. This reading aligns with @ballouPerceivedValueVideo2025, who found that players' subjective evaluations of how well gaming fits their life predicted well-being an order of magnitude more strongly than playtime itself. Similarly, in our data, the baseline well-being differences between playstyles substantially exceed any playtime-related effects. The consistent null slopes across all three playstyles thus converge with the broader accumulating evidence that quality of play determines the wellbeing outcomes, not the quantity.

What the data *do* show is that the playtime paradigm is incomplete in a critical respect: it misses qualitative differences in play that are associated with meaningful variation in average well-being. Playtime treats all minutes of play as equivalent, but players who devote their time to cooperative PVE gameplay report higher well-being than those who spend comparable time in competitive PVP modes. This between-group difference in levels—rather than in slopes—is the primary empirical contribution of the present study. In this respect, our results complement rather than contradict platform-level research: playtime may indeed be uninformative as a predictor of well-being, but the behavioural composition of that playtime is not.

Furthermore, this study addresses the fundamental trade-off in the current generation of digital trace research on games and well-being between breadth and depth. Where platform-level datasets capture play across large numbers of games, long time horizons, and diverse platforms, offering unprecedented breadth in behavioural coverage [@ballouOpenPlayLongitudinal2025; @ballouPerceivedValueVideo2025; @vuorreTimeSpentPlaying2022]. Yet these approaches typically lack behavioural granularity regarding what happens *within* a game—whether players compete or cooperate, what roles they adopt, and how they perform under different in-game demands. This matters because in-game interaction patterns are precisely the level at which many plausible well-being-relevant mechanisms operate, including social connectedness, competence experiences, and stress from competitive evaluation [@elsonMoreStoriesButtons2014]. By sacrificing the breadth of platform-level work for the depth of session-level behavioural profiling within a single title, we demonstrate what becomes visible at finer resolution: meaningful behavioural subgroups with distinct well-being correlates.

Still, neither approach alone provides a complete picture, and we see strong potential in future study designs that combine them. A platform-level dataset augmented with targeted in-game behavioural telemetry from selected titles could capture *both* the breadth of cross-game play patterns *and* the depth of within-game behavioural profiles, allowing researchers to test whether the playstyle–wellbeing associations documented here generalise beyond a single game or whether they are moderated by platform- and genre-level factors. We also see value in combining digital trace data with experimental designs that would enable stronger causal inference. For example, randomising players to specific game modes or restricting play to certain conditions to examine effects on well-being. Digital trace data is uniquely suited to complement such designs because it allows researchers to monitor adherence (whether participants follow assigned play patterns) and substitution (what games or modes participants switch to under intervention), addressing key threats to validity in field experiments on gaming behaviour.

## Limitations & Future Work

Several limitations constrain interpretation of these findings and point toward productive directions for future research. First, the study examines a single game with a specific player population recruited by the developer, and we lack evidence that the identified playstyles generalise to other games, genres, or player populations. Unlike personality traits, playstyles may be contextual and fluid, varying with game design and a player's development over time. 

Second, while our behavioural telemetry provided comprehensive metrics on core gameplay mechanics and performance, it captured only a subset of potential in-game behaviours. Notably absent were indicators of social interaction, which is an important dimension of multiplayer gaming experiences that the IMP identifies as part of the contextual layer of player experience [@elsonMoreStoriesButtons2014]. In this study, sessions spent in the game's social hub were considered idle time and thus excluded from the playstyle analysis because these sessions generated no mechanics-relevant telemetry on which to cluster. Yet recent work has shown that ostensibly idle or non-interactive periods in games, including socialising between matches and exploring social spaces, can serve experiential functions such as community-building and strategic preparation that shape the broader play experience [@tepponenUnderstandingWaitingVideo2025]. Future work could address this gap by extending the playstyle profiling framework to incorporate social and communicative dimensions of gameplay. Gesture-based digital trace data, such as the 'ping' communication systems common in multiplayer games, been demonstrated to be related to game context, team dynamics, and performance outcomes[@zhengUnderstandingPlayersGestureBased2023]. Integrating such communicative behaviours alongside signals from social spaces like the hub sessions excluded here would allow researchers to construct a more complete picture of how players engage with a game's social layer and whether these patterns relate to well-being independently of combat-focused playstyle. 

Third, the cross-sectional design precludes causal inference. The observed associations between playstyle and well-being may reflect selection effects (e.g., players with higher baseline well-being may gravitate toward cooperative play) rather than the influence of play behaviour on outcomes. Fourth, the silhouette coefficient for the three-cluster solution, while above the threshold for meaningful structure, fell at the boundary between weak and strong clustering, suggesting that playstyle variation in this game may be better described as a continuum than as discrete types. Finally, while we have proposed mechanistic interpretations through the lens of the IMP and need satisfaction theory, these remain speculative—future work should directly measure autonomy, competence, and relatedness across playstyles to test whether these mediate the observed associations.

## Conclusion

This study demonstrates that players of the same game engage with it in qualitatively different ways and that these differences are associated with meaningful variation in well-being and enjoyment. However, we found no evidence that playstyle moderates the playtime–well-being relationship. These findings contribute to digital trace research in two respects. First, they provide empirical support for the view that gameplay constitutes heterogeneous exposures with distinct psychological correlates, even within a single title. Second, they demonstrate a practical methodology, telemetry-driven profiling, for incorporating in-game behavioural depth into well-being research. At the same time, the null moderation results caution against overclaiming, the present data show that *what* players do is associated with how they feel, but not that different styles of play change *how playtime itself relates to well-being*. As richer forms of in-game data become available through industry–academic partnerships, researchers should dig deeper and continue to develop more granular, theory-driven approaches to understanding player experience and its relationship to psychological outcomes.

## Acknowledgements

Claude (Sonnet/Opus 4.6; Anthropic) was used to assist with code development and minor text edits during the preparation of this manuscript. All AI-assisted contributions were reviewed, verified, and, where necessary, further revised by members of the author team, who maintain full responsibility for the final content.

## Data Availability

All data, materials, and code related to the dataset and this manuscript are available under {{< meta data-license >}} at <https://github.com/Thomhak/playstyle-article.git>.

[funding]

## Conflicts of Interest

The authors declare no competing financial, intellectual, or institutional interests relevant to this research. The disclosures below are provided in the interest of full transparency.

No authors have received funding or compensation from the tech companies or other industries connected to the work presented here.

MV has provided in-kind consultations and served as a nonpaid panel member for Meta and K-Games. AKP has served in advisory or expert roles for the Sync Digital Wellbeing Program (2022–2024), the Google Expert Advisory on Youth and Technology (2025), the OpenAI Expert Council on Well-Being and AI (2025), and UK Department for Science, Innovation and Technology–funded research on children's smartphone and social media use (University of Cambridge, 2025). He is a member of the UK Department for Culture, Media & Sport College of Experts. Any industry fees are directly donated to charity, and his research is conducted in accordance with University of Oxford academic integrity policies.

Industry partners had no role in the design, analysis, or publication of results.

# References

:::

# Appendix

## Appendix 1: Cluster Tendency Assessment

```{r}
#| label: appendix_cluster_tendency
#| fig.width: 12
#| fig.height: 10
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig.cap: "Cluster tendency assessment comparing player data (left column) to random data (right column). Top row: Visual Assessment of Cluster Tendency (VAT) ordered dissimilarity matrices. Bottom row: PCA visualizations. The player data shows clear clustering structure (dark blocks in VAT, visible groupings in PCA) while random data shows no such patterns."

# Combine all four plots into a 2x2 grid
grid.arrange(
  dist_plot_real + labs(title = "A: VAT - Player Data"),
  dist_plot_random + labs(title = "B: VAT - Random Data"),
  pca_plot_real + labs(title = "C: PCA - Player Data"),
  pca_plot_random + labs(title = "D: PCA - Random Data"),
  ncol = 2, nrow = 2
)
```

## Appendix 2: Cluster Validation Plots

```{r}
#| label: appendix_cluster_validation_plots
#| fig.width: 6
#| fig.height: 4
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig.cap: "Cluster validation using the Calinski-Harabasz Index across k = 2 to 10."

# Display CH-index plot in appendix
p_pam_ch
```

The plot above shows the validation method used to determine the optimal number of clusters. The Calinski-Harabasz Index evaluates cluster validity based on the ratio of between-cluster to within-cluster variance, with higher values indicating better separation between clusters. The index indicated that k = 3 was the optimal number of clusters for our player data.

## Appendix 3: Statistical Tables for Interpretation

```{r}
#| label: appendix_stat_tables_prep
#| include: false
#| cache: false

fmt_p_table <- function(p) {
  case_when(
    is.na(p) ~ "NA",
    p < 0.001 ~ "< .001",
    TRUE ~ sprintf("%.3f", p)
  )
}

tidy_rlmer_table <- function(model, outcome, model_name) {
  as.data.frame(coef(summary(model))) %>%
    rownames_to_column("term") %>%
    as_tibble() %>%
    rename(
      estimate = Estimate,
      std_error = `Std. Error`,
      t_value = `t value`
    ) %>%
    mutate(
      outcome = outcome,
      model = model_name,
      p_approx = 2 * pnorm(-abs(t_value)),
      significant = p_approx < 0.05
    )
}

term_map <- c(
  "(Intercept)" = "Intercept",
  "player_typeCasual PVP Players" = "Casual PVP vs Casual PVE",
  "player_typeElite Allround Players" = "Elite Allround vs Casual PVE",
  "hours" = "Hours played",
  "genderMale" = "Gender: Male (vs Female)",
  "genderOther" = "Gender: Other (vs Female)",
  "genderPrefer not to say" = "Gender: Undisclosed (vs Female)",
  "age" = "Age",
  "player_typeCasual PVP Players:hours" = "Casual PVP × Hours",
  "player_typeElite Allround Players:hours" = "Elite Allround × Hours"
)

make_term_label <- function(term) {
  recode(term, !!!term_map, .default = term)
}

tbl_main_model <- bind_rows(
  tidy_rlmer_table(rmodel_game, "Well-being (SPANE)", "Main effects"),
  tidy_rlmer_table(rmodel_enjoyment, "Enjoyment", "Main effects")
) %>%
  mutate(term_label = make_term_label(term)) %>%
  filter(term != "(Intercept)")

tbl_moderation_model <- bind_rows(
  tidy_rlmer_table(rmodel_moderation_game, "Well-being (SPANE)", "Moderation"),
  tidy_rlmer_table(rmodel_moderation_enjoyment, "Enjoyment", "Moderation")
) %>%
  mutate(term_label = make_term_label(term)) %>%
  filter(term != "(Intercept)")

tbl_anova <- all_anova_results %>%
  filter(term == "player_type") %>%
  mutate(
    outcome = recode(
      variable,
      "spane_game_balance" = "Well-being (SPANE)",
      "enjoyment" = "Enjoyment"
    ),
    significant = p.value < 0.05
  )

tbl_posthoc <- bind_rows(
  wellbeing_posthoc %>% mutate(outcome = "Well-being (SPANE)"),
  enjoyment_posthoc %>% mutate(outcome = "Enjoyment")
) %>%
  mutate(significant = adj.p.value < 0.05)

tbl_simple_slopes <- bind_rows(
  wellbeing_slopes %>% mutate(outcome = "Well-being (SPANE)"),
  enjoyment_slopes %>% mutate(outcome = "Enjoyment")
) %>%
  mutate(significant = p.value < 0.05)

tbl_slope_pairs <- bind_rows(
  wb_pairs_numeric %>% mutate(outcome = "Well-being (SPANE)"),
  enj_pairs_numeric %>% mutate(outcome = "Enjoyment")
) %>%
  mutate(significant = p.value < 0.05)

tbl_significant_findings <- bind_rows(
  tbl_main_model %>%
    filter(significant) %>%
    transmute(
      analysis = model,
      outcome,
      test = term_label,
      effect = estimate,
      statistic = t_value,
      p_value = p_approx
    ),
  tbl_moderation_model %>%
    filter(significant) %>%
    transmute(
      analysis = model,
      outcome,
      test = term_label,
      effect = estimate,
      statistic = t_value,
      p_value = p_approx
    ),
  tbl_anova %>%
    filter(significant) %>%
    transmute(
      analysis = "ANOVA",
      outcome,
      test = "Overall play-style effect",
      effect = eta_squared,
      statistic = statistic,
      p_value = p.value
    ),
  tbl_posthoc %>%
    filter(significant) %>%
    transmute(
      analysis = "Post-hoc (Tukey HSD)",
      outcome,
      test = contrast,
      effect = estimate,
      statistic = NA_real_,
      p_value = adj.p.value
    ),
  tbl_simple_slopes %>%
    filter(significant) %>%
    transmute(
      analysis = "Simple slopes",
      outcome,
      test = player_type,
      effect = estimate,
      statistic = statistic,
      p_value = p.value
    ),
  tbl_slope_pairs %>%
    filter(significant) %>%
    transmute(
      analysis = "Pairwise slope differences",
      outcome,
      test = paste(player_type1, "vs", player_type2),
      effect = difference,
      statistic = statistic,
      p_value = p.value
    )
) %>%
  arrange(analysis, outcome)
```

```{r}
#| label: appendix_table_main_effects
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

tbl_main_model %>%
  transmute(
    Outcome = outcome,
    Term = term_label,
    Estimate = round(estimate, 3),
    SE = round(std_error, 3),
    t = round(t_value, 3),
    `Approx. p` = fmt_p_table(p_approx),
    Significant = if_else(significant, "Yes", "No")
  ) %>%
  tt(caption = "Main-Effects Robust Mixed Models (Approximate Significance)") %>%
  format_tt(digits = 3)
```

```{r}
#| label: appendix_table_moderation
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

tbl_moderation_model %>%
  transmute(
    Outcome = outcome,
    Term = term_label,
    Estimate = round(estimate, 3),
    SE = round(std_error, 3),
    t = round(t_value, 3),
    `Approx. p` = fmt_p_table(p_approx),
    Significant = if_else(significant, "Yes", "No")
  ) %>%
  tt(caption = "Moderation Models (Play Style × Hours; Approximate Significance)") %>%
  format_tt(digits = 3)
```

```{r}
#| label: appendix_table_anova_posthoc
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

tbl_anova %>%
  transmute(
    Outcome = outcome,
    `df1` = df,
    `df2` = df_residual,
    F = round(statistic, 2),
    p = fmt_p_table(p.value),
    `Eta-squared` = round(eta_squared, 3),
    Significant = if_else(significant, "Yes", "No")
  ) %>%
  tt(caption = "Omnibus ANOVA Tests by Outcome") %>%
  format_tt(digits = 3)

tbl_posthoc %>%
  transmute(
    Outcome = outcome,
    Contrast = contrast,
    Estimate = round(estimate, 3),
    `CI Low` = round(conf.low, 3),
    `CI High` = round(conf.high, 3),
    `Adj. p` = fmt_p_table(adj.p.value),
    Significant = if_else(significant, "Yes", "No")
  ) %>%
  tt(caption = "Post-hoc Tukey HSD Comparisons by Outcome") %>%
  format_tt(digits = 3)
```

```{r}
#| label: appendix_table_slopes
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

tbl_simple_slopes %>%
  transmute(
    Outcome = outcome,
    `Play Style` = player_type,
    b = round(estimate, 3),
    SE = round(std.error, 3),
    t = round(statistic, 3),
    p = fmt_p_table(p.value),
    `CI Low` = round(conf.low, 3),
    `CI High` = round(conf.high, 3),
    Significant = if_else(significant, "Yes", "No")
  ) %>%
  tt(caption = "Simple Slopes by Play Style and Outcome") %>%
  format_tt(digits = 3)

tbl_slope_pairs %>%
  transmute(
    Outcome = outcome,
    Contrast = paste(player_type1, "vs", player_type2),
    Diff = round(difference, 3),
    SE = round(std.error, 3),
    t = round(statistic, 3),
    p = fmt_p_table(p.value),
    Significant = if_else(significant, "Yes", "No")
  ) %>%
  tt(caption = "Pairwise Differences Between Simple Slopes") %>%
  format_tt(digits = 3)
```

```{r}
#| label: appendix_table_significant_findings
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

tbl_significant_findings %>%
  transmute(
    Analysis = analysis,
    Outcome = outcome,
    Test = test,
    Effect = round(effect, 3),
    Statistic = round(statistic, 3),
    p = fmt_p_table(p_value)
  ) %>%
  tt(caption = "Significant Findings Summary (p < .05)") %>%
  format_tt(digits = 3)
```

These appendix tables summarize all model terms, omnibus tests, post-hoc contrasts, simple slopes, and pairwise slope differences to support interpretation of statistically significant findings. For robust mixed-effects models (`rlmer`), p-values are reported as normal-approximation values derived from t-statistics and should be interpreted as approximate.
